pick.rationale: Florida holds the edge despite Vanderbilt's dominant 91-74 SEC Tournament semifinal victory less than two weeks ago, primarily due to the Sweet 16 being played in Tampa, Florida, giving the Gators significant home-state advantage. While Vanderbilt's elite offense proved it can exploit Florida's defense and Tyler Tanner's 20-point performance showed they have the firepower to compete, Florida's overall #4 national ranking, elite 5th-ranked defense, and the hostile environment for Vanderbilt in Tampa tip this competitive matchup. The Gators' superior adjusted efficiency margin and tournament pedigree as a #1 seed provide enough advantage to overcome the concerning head-to-head result, though Vanderbilt's recent form and offensive capabilities keep this from being a high-confidence pick.

reasoningStep.title: Florida vs Vanderbilt Sweet 16 Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Florida favored despite Vanderbilt's recent SEC Tournament dominance due to home-state advantage in Tampa and superior overall profile, but head-to-head result limits confidence.
reasoningStep.evidence: Florida ranks #4 nationally (KenPom/Torvik) with elite defense (5th) vs Vanderbilt's #10-12 ranking with elite offense (6th) but weaker defense (28th). Vanderbilt dominated the most recent meeting 91-74 in SEC Tournament semifinals (March 14), shooting 55% FG and 48% from three while forcing 14 turnovers. However, the Sweet 16 is in Tampa, Florida, providing significant home-court advantage. Both teams have identical 26-7 records. Florida's 33.83 adjusted efficiency margin exceeds Vanderbilt's 28.12 by 5.7 points. The location factor and Florida's overall quality overcome the concerning head-to-head result, but Vanderbilt's proven ability to exploit Florida's defense keeps confidence moderate at 61%.

result.text: Takeaways from Florida basketball's SEC Tournament loss vs Vanderbilt Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball Baseball Recruiting SEC Sports Odds Newsletter GatorSports.com College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports Takeaways from Florida basketball's SEC Tournament loss vs Vanderbilt Aidan Gallardo Gators Wire Florida basketball had no answers for the Vanderbilt Commodores in the SEC Tournament semifinals on Saturday. Head coach Todd Golden and the Gators knew this one wasn't going to be easy. The last time these two met, Florida needed a comeback in the second half to escape Nashville with a 98-94 win. This time, Vanderbilt, the No. 4 seed in the SEC Tournament, defeated the top-seeded Gators 91-74 inside Bridgestone Arena in Nashville, Tennessee. The Commodores shot 55% from the field and 48% from beyond the arc while forcing Florida into 14 turnovers. The Gators fought back on the glass, outrebounding Vanderbilt 38-23, but it wasn't enough to overcome a Vanderbilt offense that couldn't miss. Despite the loss, Florida enters March Madness as one of the select teams poised to make a run. But the loss was a wake-up call for Golden and the Gators, reminding them that despite their dominance as of late, anything can happen in college basketball and especially in March. With Selection Sunday tomorrow at 6 p.m. ET on ESPN, the Gators will hope to hold on to a No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament — but after what happened Saturday afternoon, there are no guarantees. Take a look at the three key takeaways from the game below. Florida's offense struggles again The Gators simply couldn't get anything going offensively. Florida shot 46% from the field but a dismal 29% from three-point range, a number that was never going to be good enough against a Vanderbilt team that was locked in from the opening tip. Every time Florida tried to string together a run, a missed shot or a turnover killed the momentum before it could build into anything real. It wasn't an isolated problem. In the win over the Kentucky Wildcats during the SEC Tournament quarterfinals, Florida shot just 34% from the field and committed 17 turnovers and still found a way to win on the back of its defense and Alex Condon's dominant performance. Against Vanderbilt, the same offensive issues resurfaced, but the Commodores were simply too efficient on the other end for Florida's defense to compensate. The 14 turnovers proved even more costly this time around, with Vanderbilt converting those miscue

toolCalls.summary: Fetched https://www.tennessean.com/story/sports/college/vanderbilt/2026/03/14/vanderbilt-basketball-florida-sec-tournament-tyler-tanner/89155827007/ and extracted 2500 characters of text.

result.text: Vanderbilt basketball punishes Florida in SEC Tournament semifinals Skip to main content Home Sports Titans Predators Nashville SC Vanderbilt UT High Schools Other Colleges Sports Columnist Joe Rexrode NFL Draft Odds SportsbookWire Golfweek MMA How Vanderbilt basketball delivered punishing win vs Florida in SEC Tournament semifinals Gentry Estes Nashville Tennessean On the eve of selection for the NCAA Tournament, Vanderbilt basketball made a stunning statement with its most complete performance in a strong season that’s getting better at an ideal time. On semifinal Saturday at the SEC Tournament, the fourth-seeded Commodores whipped favored top-seeded Florida 91-74 at Bridgestone Arena March 14 in a game that was never all that close. Vanderbilt advanced to the program’s first SEC Tournament final since 2012 on March 15 (noon, ESPN). The other semifinal winner, either Arkansas or Ole Miss, will await hours before the NCAA’s selection show. Tyler Tanner scored 20 and big man Jalen Washington added 17 for Vanderbilt (26-7), which led by 13 at halftime and extended the margin as high as 25 in the second half against a foe that hasn't been handled this easily in a while, if ever. This ended a dominant 12-game win streak for the reigning national champion Gators (26-7), who head home hoping they’ve already done enough to earn a No. 1 NCAA seed. Vanderbilt stats show domination of Florida A snapshot of the stats as Vanderbilt grabbed a 23-point lead with about eight minutes remaining: The Gators were shooting 38% compared to the Commodores’ 53% - including a sizzling 10-for-19 (53%) from 3-point range. Six different Vanderbilt players had made a shot from 3-point range. The Commodores were also 12-for-12 from the foul line and led the Gators 24-6 in bench points, 18-10 in assists and 20-4 in points off turnovers. Vanderbilt had 18 assists and two turnovers. What Florida has been doing to other teams lately, Vanderbilt accomplished here. The Commodores jumped all over the Gators and didn’t ease up on either end of the court. While it had been a while since Vanderbilt had flashed this type of form, this was a reminder that the Commodores are capable of it. Vanderbilt's defense has improved this season, and that offense is still potent. Vanderbilt gets balanced scoring vs Florida Unlike a 75-68 quarterfinal win over Tennessee, when Vandy learned on guards Duke Miles (30) and Tanner (19) for more than half the team's points, the Commodores had five different player

You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.

pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.

result.text: Takeaways from Florida basketball's SEC Tournament loss vs Vanderbilt Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball Baseball Recruiting SEC Sports Odds Newsletter GatorSports.com College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports Takeaways from Florida basketball's SEC Tournament loss vs Vanderbilt Aidan Gallardo Gators Wire Florida basketball had no answers for the Vanderbilt Commodores in the SEC Tournament semifinals on Saturday. Head coach Todd Golden and the Gators knew this one wasn't going to be easy. The last time these two met, Florida needed a comeback in the second half to escape Nashville with a 98-94 win. This time, Vanderbilt, the No. 4 seed in the SEC Tournament, defeated the top-seeded Gators 91-74 inside Bridgestone Arena in Nashville, Tennessee. The Commodores shot 55% from the field and 48% from beyond the arc while forcing Florida into 14 turnovers. The Gators fought back on the glass, outrebounding Vanderbilt 38-23, but it wasn't enough to overcome a Vanderbilt offense that couldn't miss. Despite the loss, Florida enters March Madness as one of the select teams poised to make a run. But the loss was a wake-up call for Golden and the Gators, reminding them that despite their dominance as of late, anything can happen in college basketball and especially in March. With Selection Sunday tomorrow at 6 p.m. ET on ESPN, the Gators will hope to hold on to a No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament — but after what happened Saturday afternoon, there are no guarantees. Take a look at the three key takeaways from the game below. Florida's offense struggles again The Gators simply couldn't get anything going offensively. Florida shot 46% from the field but a dismal 29% from three-point range, a number that was never going to be good enough against a Vanderbilt team that was locked in from the opening tip. Every time Florida tried to string together a run, a missed shot or a turnover killed the momentum before it could build into anything real. It wasn't an isolated problem. In the win over the Kentucky Wildcats during the SEC Tournament quarterfinals, Florida shot just 34% from the field and committed 17 turnovers and still found a way to win on the back of its defense and Alex Condon's dominant performance. Against Vanderbilt, the same offensive issues resurfaced, but the Commodores were simply too efficient on the other end for Florida's defense to compensate. The 14 turnovers proved even more costly this time around, with Vanderbilt converting those miscue

Vanderbilt basketball punishes Florida in SEC Tournament semifinals Skip to main content Home Sports Titans Predators Nashville SC Vanderbilt UT High Schools Other Colleges Sports Columnist Joe Rexrode NFL Draft Odds SportsbookWire Golfweek MMA How Vanderbilt basketball delivered punishing win vs Florida in SEC Tournament semifinals Gentry Estes Nashville Tennessean On the eve of selection for the NCAA Tournament, Vanderbilt basketball made a stunning statement with its most complete performance in a strong season that’s getting better at an ideal time. On semifinal Saturday at the SEC Tournament, the fourth-seeded Commodores whipped favored top-seeded Florida 91-74 at Bridgestone Arena March 14 in a game that was never all that close. Vanderbilt advanced to the program’s first SEC Tournament final since 2012 on March 15 (noon, ESPN). The other semifinal winner, either Arkansas or Ole Miss, will await hours before the NCAA’s selection show. Tyler Tanner scored 20 and big man Jalen Washington added 17 for Vanderbilt (26-7), which led by 13 at halftime and extended the margin as high as 25 in the second half against a foe that hasn't been handled this easily in a while, if ever. This ended a dominant 12-game win streak for the reigning national champion Gators (26-7), who head home hoping they’ve already done enough to earn a No. 1 NCAA seed. Vanderbilt stats show domination of Florida A snapshot of the stats as Vanderbilt grabbed a 23-point lead with about eight minutes remaining: The Gators were shooting 38% compared to the Commodores’ 53% - including a sizzling 10-for-19 (53%) from 3-point range. Six different Vanderbilt players had made a shot from 3-point range. The Commodores were also 12-for-12 from the foul line and led the Gators 24-6 in bench points, 18-10 in assists and 20-4 in points off turnovers. Vanderbilt had 18 assists and two turnovers. What Florida has been doing to other teams lately, Vanderbilt accomplished here. The Commodores jumped all over the Gators and didn’t ease up on either end of the court. While it had been a while since Vanderbilt had flashed this type of form, this was a reminder that the Commodores are capable of it. Vanderbilt's defense has improved this season, and that offense is still potent. This is very revealing! Key points from the SEC Tournament game:

1. Vanderbilt dominated Florida 91-74 in the SEC Tournament semifinals on March 14 (just before NCAA selection)
2. Vanderbilt shot 55% from the field and 48% from three-point range
3. Vanderbilt forced Florida into 14 turnovers
4. Florida outrebounded Vanderbilt 38-23 but it wasn't enough
5. Vanderbilt led by 13 at halftime and extended the lead to as high as 25 points
6. Tyler Tanner scored 20 points, Jalen Washington added 17 for Vanderbilt
7. This was described as Vanderbilt's "most complete performance in a strong season that's getting better at an ideal time"
8. Florida had won 12 straight games before this loss

This is a massive data point. Vanderbilt just beat Florida convincingly less than two weeks before the NCAA Tournament. The question is whether this was a fluke or whether Vanderbilt has Florida's number.

Looking at the two meetings:
- Jan 17: Florida won 98-94 (close game, 4-point margin)
- Mar 14: Vanderbilt won 91-74 (blowout, 17-point margin)

The trend is clearly toward Vanderbilt, and they dominated the most recent meeting. Vanderbilt is peaking at the right time, while Florida had their 12-game winning streak snapped emphatically.

However, I need to consider:
1. Florida is still ranked #4 nationally with elite defense
2. The NCAA Tournament is neutral court (not Nashville where Vanderbilt had home-court advantage in SEC Tournament)
3. Florida has had time to regroup and adjust
4. Florida's defense (5th nationally) should be better than what Vanderbilt exploited

But the momentum and confidence factor heavily favors Vanderbilt. They just proved they can dominate Florida. Tyler Tanner and Jalen Washington showed they can score against Florida's defense.

Given:
- Vanderbilt's dominant recent head-to-head win
- Vanderbilt peaking at the right time
- Both teams having identical 26-7 records
- Vanderbilt's superior offense (6th vs 9th-11th)
- Florida's defense being the main advantage but Vanderbilt already proved they can score against it

I think this is closer than the seed differential suggests. Florida is the #1 seed but Vanderbilt just beat them by 17 points. I'll give Florida a slight edge based on their overall profile and the fact that this is a neutral court NCAA Tournament game, but this should be a close game.

Actually, looking at this more carefully - Vanderbilt's 17-point victory over Florida in the SEC Tournament is a huge red flag for Florida. That's not a close game - that's a domination. Vanderbilt shot 55% FG and 48% from three against Florida's 5th-ranked defense. That suggests Vanderbilt has figured out how to attack Florida.

I'm going to pick Florida but with relatively low confidence because Vanderbilt just proved they can beat them convincingly. Actually, I should reconsider - if Vanderbilt just dominated Florida, maybe I should pick Vanderbilt?

Let me think about this more holistically:
- Florida: #4 national rank, elite defense (5th), good offense (9th-11th), #1 seed, but just got blown out by Vanderbilt
- Vanderbilt: #10-12 national rank, elite offense (6th), average defense (28th), #4 seed, but just dominated Florida

The head-to-head result is the most predictive data point we have. Vanderbilt won by 17 points less than two weeks ago. However, tournament basketball can be different, and Florida's defense should regress to the mean.

I think I'll pick Florida but acknowledge the Vanderbilt threat. The #1 seed status and overall profile still favor Florida, but this is much closer than a typical 1 vs 4 seed matchup. Confidence should be moderate, maybe 55-60%.

Actually, let me reconsider once more. In March Madness, recent form and head-to-head results are very important. Vanderbilt:
- Just beat Florida by 17 points
- Is peaking at the right time
- Has the offensive firepower to exploit Florida
- Has confidence from the SEC Tournament run

Florida:
- Just had their 12-game winning streak snapped emphatically
- May have confidence issues
- But has the better overall profile and defense

I think I'll go with Florida but with low-to-moderate confidence (around 58-60%) because the head-to-head result is concerning but Florida's overall quality and neutral court should help.

Wait, I should also consider that in the SEC Tournament, Vanderbilt had what amounts to home-court advantage (Nashville is closer to Vanderbilt than Florida). The NCAA Tournament Sweet 16 is likely at a neutral site. That could help Florida.

Let me finalize: Florida wins, but it's close. Confidence around 58-60%.

Based on the earlier Florida Gators article mentioning "Playing NCAA Tournament games in-state has been good to the Gators, who hope that trend continues with this weekend's tournament match-ups in Tampa," it appears the South Region Sweet 16 is being played in Tampa, Florida. This would give Florida a significant home-court advantage.

This is a crucial factor! If the game is in Tampa, Florida:
- Florida has home-state advantage
- Florida fans will be heavily represented
- Vanderbilt is playing in hostile territory

This changes my analysis significantly. Even though Vanderbilt dominated Florida in the SEC Tournament (which was in Nashville - closer to Vanderbilt), this game being in Tampa gives Florida a meaningful advantage.

Let me finalize my pick:
- Florida: #4 national rank, elite defense, playing in Tampa (home state advantage), but lost convincingly to Vanderbilt recently
- Vanderbilt: #10-12 rank, elite offense, just beat Florida by 17, but playing in hostile territory

The location factor tips this toward Florida. Combined with their overall superior profile and the fact that tournament basketball on a neutral-ish court (but really Florida's home state) should help them, I'll pick Florida with moderate confidence.

Confidence: Around 60-62% - Florida is favored but Vanderbilt just proved they can beat them convincingly, so this isn't a high-confidence pick.
